title: Modern R for Public Health Practitioners
text:
Welcome
This is the website for the Connecticut Department of Public Health’s Data Management, Analytics, and Governance (DMAG) R training for public health practitioners.

title: Modern R for Public Health Practitioners
text:
Special Thanks

Gary Archambault and the rest of the DMAG team
Mukhtar Mohamed
Sydney Jones

title: Modern R for Public Health Practitioners
text:
About the Author
Epidemiologist turned Data Scientist with a decade of R experience.

title: 1  Adopting an R Project Mindset
text: 1.1 The Mental Model
crumbs: 1  Adopting an R Project Mindset

title: 2  How to use this book
text:
2.1 The big idea
This course is not about teaching you epidemiology. It’s about giving you the R fluency to apply what you already know.
We will dive straight into a data analysis project and with every chapter will we iteratively polish our analysis until at the very end we have something that could be confidently deployed in day to day use.
To get the most out of the lessons users should code along with the examples.
crumbs: 2  How to use this book

title: 2  How to use this book
text:
2.2 The arc
WIP - PLACEHOLDER








Chapter
What you build
The new skill




0 — Orientation
Your first rendered R analysis
R mindset; the SAS→R Rosetta stone


1 — Import
A real CT dataset + data profile
readr; inspect before you trust


2 — Wrangling
A clean, joined, analysis-ready dataset
tidyverse DATA-step verbs; joins


3 — Descriptive epi
Publication-quality tables
gt; counts, rates, CIs


4 — Visualization
Trend charts + a CT choropleth map
ggplot2; sf + tigris


5 — Quarto report
Leadership-ready report v1
Quarto; findings before methods


6 — Functions
One template, any county or year
Functions; Quarto params


7 — API
A self-fetching, live-data report
SODA API; SoQL; fallback caching


8 — Reproducibility
Published GitHub Pages capstone
renv; here; GitHub Pages


9 — Automation (stretch)
Scheduled re-render
GitHub Actions; handoff
crumbs: 2  How to use this book

title: 2  How to use this book
text:
2.3 How to use this site
Work through modules in order. Each one has:

Overview — what you’ll build, why it matters, and the SAS concepts it remaps
Lesson — a runnable, narrated .qmd file you execute yourself
Exercises — 3–5 scaffolded problems with explicit “done when” checks
Solutions — available after you’ve tried on your own
crumbs: 2  How to use this book

title: 3  Getting Ready for working with files
text:
3.1 The Setup
Covid-19 challenged Public Health infrastructure in so many multi-faceted ways. The sheer amount of data collected and processed over the pandemic meant that what worked in the beginning of the pandemic was no longer feasible towards the end of the pandemic.
Over the course of this book we will explore Connecticut pandemic data and how to deal with the ever increasing amount of data. We will start simple and do things manually before moving on to refine our analytics methodologies.
crumbs: 3  Getting Ready for working with files

title: 3  Getting Ready for working with files
text:
3.2 Data Intro
We are going to use a snippet of real pandemic-era covid-19 data from the connecticut department of public health’s open data portal1.
The full dataset can be found on the portal2 but we will start simply and use the provided snippet in this
TODO: add evergreen link
repository chapters/working-with-real-data/data/covid-2022.csv
crumbs: 3  Getting Ready for working with files

title: 3  Getting Ready for working with files
text:
3.3 Setup
R has many tools for working with data. Over the years the R community has built up a number of their own tools and one of the most popular, the tidyverse will be our entrypoint into working with data in R.
R comes with a base set of tools and eager epidemiologists can opt to use only the base suite of tool and go out into the world if they so choose. However, many smart developers and epidemiologists have come before us and we do not need to recreate the wheel. R has what are called `packages’ - or collection of ready made code that folks can use to get work done.
TODO: – talk about the CRAN etc etc….
We’re going to slowly introduce the different parts of the tidyverse individually….
TODO: flesh

3.3.1 Installing Packages

# pulls the code down from the CRAN for folks to use and installs on 
# their computer
install.packages(
    "dplyr",  # for manipulating data
    "readr", # for reading data files from a users' computers
    "fs" # for working with files on users' file systems
    )
crumbs: 3  Getting Ready for working with files

title: 4  Reading Comma Delimited Files
text:
4.1 Reading our Comma Delimited File (.csv)
We are going to do a few things here:
# Users can have all sorts of packages on their computer
# but will only need a limited subset of them at any one time
# add these packages to your session with `library()` function.

library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(fs)

# we have a file - how do we read it in?
# we use fs's path() function to create a path
# we user readr's read_csv() and point it to  our path

csv_path <- path("data/covid-2022.csv")

covid_data <- read_csv(csv_path)
We have our data saved into our session, now what? Let’s take a look at it!
# taking a look at the top few rows
head(covid_data)

# A tibble: 6 × 16

  <date>                 <dbl> <chr>        <dbl>           <dbl>          <dbl>

2 2022-01-03                 2 Anson…        3103            2632            471
3 2022-01-03                 3 Ashfo…         441             398             43
4 2022-01-03                 4 Avon          1690            1584            106
5 2022-01-03                 5 Barkh…         325             309             16
6 2022-01-03                 6 Beaco…         888             790             98

#   number_of_positives <dbl>, number_of_negatives <dbl>,
#   number_of_indeterminates <dbl>
# glimpse from dplyr
glimpse(covid_data)

Rows: 20,956

title: 4  Reading Comma Delimited Files
text:
‘Invoke’ the packages we will be using so they are availabe to us.
Create and capture the path to our data file and save inside of an object csv_path.
Read the data into our session from the csv and save inside of an object covid_data.

title: 5  Working with APIs and Functions
text:
5.1 What is an API!?
To put it simply, An API1 or application programming interface can be thought of in part as another tool in the user’s tool belt to programmatically interact and often extract data without having to manually download data. No one needs to lift their mouse and search through a webpage and click download. This is a simple, yet powerful concept that extend to many areas of data work and beyond. I encourage users to read up on and develop their API skills beyond what is presented in our coursework.
crumbs: 5  Working with APIs and Functions

title: 5  Working with APIs and Functions
text:
5.2 Working with httr2
We will be working with httr2 as our package of choice for API work. Often, specific APIs will have their own packages for working with them, it is often better to use those, but we cannot cover them all, and not every API has its own package. httr2 will always be here for us, and if users can understand httr2 then picking up the other more specific packages will become even easier.
Remember our Covid-19 pandemic dataset? Before we would navigate to that website and download the datafile, or perhaps rely on the snippet of data provided in this course. No more - we are far too busy for that kind of work. From here on out we will be using the API, and in the future when users encounter data they want to incorporate into their analyses - it is almost always better to use the API.
Install httr2 like we would any other package.

install.packages('httr2')

Next we’ll attach the httr2 and dplyr packages to our session.

library(dplyr) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages() # this silences dplyr messages when loading the package
library(httr2)


5.2.1 httr2 requests and responses
Recall our pandemic data’s landing page - notice what pops up when you click the ‘Export’ button in the top right of the webpage. The popup has ‘Download file’ defaulting to ‘Export format’ of csv. However, right next to it is ‘API endpoint’ - click that and notice the ‘Data format’ switches to ‘JSON’. Below that you may notice that there is a ‘Version’ toggle for either ‘SODA3’ or ‘SODA2’. For now, select ‘SODA2’ and copy the url provided, it should look like this: https://data.ct.gov/resource/28fr-iqnx.json now that we have our url or endpoint as they are often called let’s hop into the code.

# save our url into an object so we don't have to type it out everywhere
endpoint <- "https://data.ct.gov/resource/28fr-iqnx.json"

# We build our data request up piece by piece

my_request <- request(endpoint) # base of the request

# Get a our response from the Open Data Portal API

odp_response <-
  my_request |>
  req_perform() # perform the request, and get a response base

odp_response

<httr2_response>
GET https://data.ct.gov/resource/28fr-iqnx.json
Status: 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Body: In memory (412447 bytes)




5.2.2 Extracting data from responses
We have a response - now where is our data? We need to pull it out of the body of the response and then convert it into an R dataframe.

my_odp_data <-
  odp_response |>
  resp_body_json() |> # extract the data, it's returned as an R list
  bind_rows() # for this endpoint we can easily convert to a dataframe

my_odp_data

# A tibble: 1,000 × 16
   lastupdatedate          town_no town        towntotalcases townconfirmedcases
   <chr>                   <chr>   <chr>       <chr>          <chr>             
 1 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 1       Andover     514            439               
 2 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 2       Ansonia     4911           4222              
 3 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 3       Ashford     744            671               
 4 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 4       Avon        3030           2773              
 5 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 5       Barkhamsted 527            490               
 6 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 6       Beacon Fal… 1306           1163              
 7 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 7       Berlin      4098           3751              
 8 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 8       Bethany     991            901               
 9 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 9       Bethel      4090           3345              
10 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 10      Bethlehem   620            529               
# ℹ 990 more rows
# ℹ 11 more variables: townprobablecases <chr>, towncaserate <chr>,
#   towntotaldeaths <chr>, townconfirmeddeaths <chr>, townprobabledeaths <chr>,
#   peopletested <chr>, ratetested100k <chr>, numberoftests <chr>,
#   numberofpositives <chr>, numberofnegatives <chr>,
#   numberofindeterminates <chr>


Lovely - but how come there are only 1000 rows? That leads us to our next wrinkle when working with APIs. This one is public, so just about anyone can hammer on this API at anytime, and some of data can be quite large. In order to prevent users from accidentally running a massive query for data they may not even need (and to prevent misuse from unscrupulous types) APIs will often only send a small chunk of the data and then offer up ways to retrieve the next chunk, and then the next chunk, and so on and so forth. This can be accomplished in a few different ways, but for this API they use pages with limits and offsets.






Note



Users working with different APIs should read that API’s documentation to see how that specific API handles pagination/iteration through responses.
crumbs: 5  Working with APIs and Functions

title: 5  Working with APIs and Functions
text:
5.3 Extracting all data
We’re going to have to do a little leg work here to get all the data, and then I am going to show you how you can package this all up so you can reuse it over and over again.
First, users need to understand what the limit and offset are and how they can leverage those to get the data they expect.

limit: The total number of rows of data brought back in the request.

The default for this API and this endpoint is 1000. Some datasets might be small like 5000 rows. You can just bump this number up to 5000 and pull it all in one go.

offset: The starting point row number for the query.

The default for this API is 0, because it starts at the first row.


These two parameters can be used in conjunction to, for example, grab the first 1000 rows of a dataset, and then grab the next 1000 and so on and so forth until all data is had.

5.3.1 Modifying the limit and offset
So modifying the limit and offset is all well in good, but how do we actually go about changing those values? Remember our endpoint url? Thankfully these are parameters that can be tacked on to the end of the url.
If for whatever reason we wanted the 10th row, and only the 10th row of our dataset we can modify our url to look like this:
https://data.ct.gov/resource/28fr-iqnx.json?$limit=1&$offset=9
Notice that the parameters come at the end of the original url after a ?. Users then append $limit= and & and $offset= with the desired values. Feel free to try this in your web browser - most modern browsers will display the data from the response.






Note



Note that items/data are zero-indexed, meaning the first item the data is ‘row’ 0. To grab the 10th item we skip the first 9 items.


Do not take my word for it. Try this out: click on https://data.ct.gov/resource/28fr-iqnx.json?$limit=10&$offset=0 and make note of the tenth item.
It should look something like this:
{
    "lastupdatedate":"2022-06-24T00:00:00.000",
    "town_no":"10","town":"Bethlehem",
    "towntotalcases":"620",
    "townconfirmedcases":"529",
    "townprobablecases":"91",
    "towncaserate":"18225",
    "towntotaldeaths":"3",
    "townconfirmeddeaths":"2",
    "townprobabledeaths":"1",
    "peopletested":"2781",
    "ratetested100k":"81746",
    "numberoftests":"11841",
    "numberofpositives":"749",
    "numberofnegatives":"11070",
    "numberofindeterminates":"22"
}
Does that match our original single item we had before?


5.3.2 Using httr2
No one wants to modify urls by hand, and we’re still clicking through everything, time to use httr2 to do the heavy lifting.

# We're going to grab first 5000 rows, 1000 rows at a time.
# so we will query 1000 rows, 5 times

req <-
  request(endpoint) |>
  # don't forget the $ as part of the parameter name for this api
  # backticks are required for nonstandard r names
  req_url_query(
    `$limit` = 1000,
    `$offset` = 0
  )


resps <-
  req_perform_iterative(
    req,
    next_req = iterate_with_offset(
      param_name = "$offset",
      start = 0,
      offset = 1000
    ),
    max_reqs = 5
  )

# resps contains a list of 5 responses
# we want to take each response and do what we did above to all of them
# we will use an anonymous function otherwise known as lambda functions in
# other programming languages

data <-
  resps |>
  resps_data(
    \(resp) {
      # declare the name of each item in our resps list
      resp |> # for each response in our responses
        resp_body_json() |> # convert the json to a list
        bind_rows() # bind these into a dataframe
    }
  )

data

# A tibble: 5,000 × 16
   lastupdatedate          town_no town        towntotalcases townconfirmedcases
   <chr>                   <chr>   <chr>       <chr>          <chr>             
 1 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 1       Andover     514            439               
 2 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 2       Ansonia     4911           4222              
 3 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 3       Ashford     744            671               
 4 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 4       Avon        3030           2773              
 5 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 5       Barkhamsted 527            490               
 6 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 6       Beacon Fal… 1306           1163              
 7 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 7       Berlin      4098           3751              
 8 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 8       Bethany     991            901               
 9 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 9       Bethel      4090           3345              
10 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 10      Bethlehem   620            529               
# ℹ 4,990 more rows
# ℹ 11 more variables: townprobablecases <chr>, towncaserate <chr>,
#   towntotaldeaths <chr>, townconfirmeddeaths <chr>, townprobabledeaths <chr>,
#   peopletested <chr>, ratetested100k <chr>, numberoftests <chr>,
#   numberofpositives <chr>, numberofnegatives <chr>,
#   numberofindeterminates <chr>




5.3.3 All the data everywhere, all at once
Wait - weren’t we promised all the data!? Fair point, how do we grab that data if we don’t know how many rows there are? At time of writing there are 102245 rows in this dataset, but let’s pretend we do not know that, and often that is the case.

# bump this up to 10k rows per request and 10k offset each times
number_of_rows <- 10000

# first request grabs the first 10k
req <-
  request(endpoint) |>
  req_url_query(
    `$limit` = number_of_rows,
    `$offset` = 0
  )

# drop the max_reqs argument
resps <-
  req_perform_iterative(
    req,
    progress = FALSE, # the progress bar is super cool, but doesn't play nicely with the website, feel free to leave it on!
    next_req = iterate_with_offset(
      param_name = "$offset",
      start = 0,
      offset = number_of_rows
    )
  )

data <-
  resps |>
  resps_data(
    \(resp) {
      resp |>
        resp_body_json() |>
        bind_rows()
    }
  )

nrow(data) == 102245

[1] TRUE


Look at that! We have our 102245 rows.






Warning



Start small when working with a new endpoint
Users will often want all the data, but they will often not know how many rows there are. Use caution when grabbing everything, there may be more data than can be handled at once.
crumbs: 5  Working with APIs and Functions

title: 5  Working with APIs and Functions
text:
5.4 Functions crash course
By now users may have noticed that we’ve written the same or similar chain of request and response a few times now. Let us tidy this up into something we can reuse over and over - a function! The httr2 documentation has a wonderful section on wrapping apis into useful functions that I will encourage users to read.
We will take much of the logic and code discussed in that document and apply it to our usecases.
Up to this point users will be using functions all over the place in R. Now we are going to write out own using the function() function to encapsulate our logic. We will do this time and time again. We create our function by providing the inputs it expects, and then perform the actions we want it to do with those inputs. Good functions typically will do one thing or step really well, and have a descriptive name. It is usually good to create and chain a few smaller functions instead of having one massive function.
Let’s start by creating a function that reduces the amount of repetitive typing we need to do, smooths over some API rough edges and finally returns all the data.
Take one more look at our endpoint: https://data.ct.gov/resource/28fr-iqnx.json. Notice anything? If we’re always using the Open Data Portal, the domain (data.ct.gov) is always going to be the same. There is always going to be a ‘/resource/’ section and always a ‘.json’. The only real part that is going to change is the resource id (often called a four by four on this portal). So that is probably a good target for input from users.

# our function will be called fetch all data
fetch_all_data <-
  function(resource, rows = 10000) {
    # Fetch all data from the provided resource id for the CT Open Data Portal
    #
    # resource: string - Required portal "four by four" in the format 'xxxx-xxxx'
    # rows: numeric - Number of rows of data to fetch one response at a time.
    #   defaults to 100000 and becomes the LIMIT and OFFSET for the request.
    # Returns: A tibble

    # handle the resource from the user

    base <- "https://data.ct.gov/resource"

    # create the first request
    req <-
      request(base) |>
      req_url_path_append(resource) |> #appened the user provided 4x4 to the base url
      req_url_query(
        `$limit` = rows, # use the default rows=10000 or what ever the user provided
        `$offset` = 0 # start with the first row
      )

    # perform the request and then with each request increment the offset by `rows`
    resps <-
      req_perform_iterative(
        req,
        progress = FALSE,
        next_req = iterate_with_offset(
          param_name = "$offset",
          start = 0,
          offset = rows # use the default rows=10000 or what ever the user provided
        )
      )

    # convert the response objects from the API to a tibble of data
    # for each response from the API grab the json body, convert to an R list
    # and then squash all that into a singular tibble
    data <-
      resps |>
      resps_data(
        \(resp) {
          resp |>
            resp_body_json() |>
            bind_rows()
        }
      )

    return(data)
  }

We wrote our function, now let us take it for a spin. Our resource ID was 28fr-iqnx.

resource <- "28fr-iqnx"
covid_data <- fetch_all_data(resource = resource)

covid_data

# A tibble: 102,245 × 16
   lastupdatedate          town_no town        towntotalcases townconfirmedcases
   <chr>                   <chr>   <chr>       <chr>          <chr>             
 1 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 1       Andover     514            439               
 2 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 2       Ansonia     4911           4222              
 3 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 3       Ashford     744            671               
 4 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 4       Avon        3030           2773              
 5 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 5       Barkhamsted 527            490               
 6 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 6       Beacon Fal… 1306           1163              
 7 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 7       Berlin      4098           3751              
 8 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 8       Bethany     991            901               
 9 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 9       Bethel      4090           3345              
10 2022-06-24T00:00:00.000 10      Bethlehem   620            529               
# ℹ 102,235 more rows
# ℹ 11 more variables: townprobablecases <chr>, towncaserate <chr>,
#   towntotaldeaths <chr>, townconfirmeddeaths <chr>, townprobabledeaths <chr>,
#   peopletested <chr>, ratetested100k <chr>, numberoftests <chr>,
#   numberofpositives <chr>, numberofnegatives <chr>,
#   numberofindeterminates <chr>


If this was your first function, congratulations!






Tip



From here users can go in many different directions. Some tips to consider: - It would be a good idea to check the user input to make sure it is valid - Handling errors gracefully would be a good next stop - remember the issue may not be on the user’s end, the portal could be having issues etc…


Users who checked out the httr2 documentation may have noticed that the authors went through great lengths to discuss secrets and api keys. Since the API key is sort of a nice to have for this public API we will be discussing those later on. See Section 7.2 for those details if you wish skip ahead now.
crumbs: 5  Working with APIs and Functions

title: 5  Working with APIs and Functions
text: IBM’s definition↩︎
crumbs: 5  Working with APIs and Functions

title: Implementing APIs into our Analysis
text:
# === setup ===
library(dplyr)
library(httr2)

# === constants ===
RESOURCE <- "28fr-iqnx"

# === functions ===
fetch_all_data <-
  function(resource, rows = 10000) {
    # Fetch all data from the provided resource id for the CT Open Data Portal
    #
    # resource: string - Required portal "four by four" in the format 'xxxx-xxxx'
    # rows: numeric - Number of rows of data to fetch one response at a time.
    #   defaults to 100000 and becomes the LIMIT and OFFSET for the request.
    # Returns: A tibble

    # handle the resource from the user

    base <- "https://data.ct.gov/resource"

    # create the first request
    req <-
      request(base) |>
      req_url_path_append(resource) |> #appened the user provided 4x4 to the base url
      req_url_query(
        `$limit` = rows, # use the default rows=10000 or what ever the user provided
        `$offset` = 0 # start with the first row
      )

    # perform the request and then with each request increment the offset by `rows`
    resps <-
      req_perform_iterative(
        req,
        progress = FALSE,
        next_req = iterate_with_offset(
          param_name = "$offset",
          start = 0,
          offset = rows # use the default rows=10000 or what ever the user provided
        )
      )

    # convert the response objects from the API to a tibble of data
    # for each response from the API grab the json body, convert to an R list
    # and then squash all that into a singular tibble
    data <-
      resps |>
      resps_data(
        \(resp) {
          resp |>
            resp_body_json() |>
            bind_rows()
        }
      )

    return(data)
  }

# === main ===
main <- function() {
  # main entrypoint into our analysis

  # data ingestion
  data <- fetch_all_data(resource = RESOURCE)

  # key measures
  total_tests <-
    data |>
    reframe(
      total_pandemic_tests = sum(
        as.numeric(numberoftests),
        na.rm = TRUE
      )
    ) |>
    pull(total_pandemic_tests)

  cat(
    paste(
      "The total number of pandemic tests in these data were:",
      format(total_tests, big.mark = ',')
    )
  )
}

# === entrypoint ===
main()

The total number of pandemic tests in these data were: 4,502,905,950

One of the authors, Hadley Wickham, is the creator of the tidyverse and this is a wonderful resource for learning analytics in R. Don’t let the Data Science in the title intimidate, there is rich info in this for R users of all skill levels.
